skill.name: biopython-entrez
skill.description: Use Bio.Entrez to access NCBI databases (e.g., PubMed/GenBank) for searching, fetching summaries, and downloading records when your workflow needs to call the NCBI E-utilities API over the network.
skill.summary: Access NCBI biological databases programmatically using Biopython's Entrez module for search, fetch, and cross-reference operations.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 126 static analysis findings were evaluated as false positives in context. The 93 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are markdown code blocks (triple backticks) in SKILL.md and references/databases.md, not actual shell commands. The 7 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are caused by the word 'Entrez' matching a crypto detection heuristic -- no cryptographic operations exist. The 9 'generic API/secret keys' findings reference legitimate NCBI E-utilities API key configuration, which is required for the skill's intended purpose. The heuristic obfuscation findings combine unrelated patterns from documentation code examples. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious behavior was detected. The skill is documentation for legitimate bioinformatics API access.

high_findings.title: API key configuration in documentation examples
high_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: SKILL.md and references/databases.md contain examples showing NCBI API key configuration via Entrez.api_key. This is expected behavior for the Bio.Entrez skill. The examples use placeholder values ('your_api_key_here') and config-file-based patterns, not hardcoded secrets. NCBI requires API keys for increased rate limits. Confidence: 0.95. Confidence reasoning: The API key usage is clearly documented as required by NCBI and follows best practices with config-file-based patterns.

medium_findings.title: Markdown code blocks flagged as shell execution
medium_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: 93 findings flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' but all are standard markdown code blocks delimited by triple backticks in SKILL.md and references/databases.md. These are Python code examples showing Bio.Entrez usage, not shell commands. No actual shell execution occurs. Confidence: 0.99. Confidence reasoning: Visual inspection confirms triple-backtick markdown fences on every flagged line in both documentation files.

medium_findings.title: File system operations in code examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE but EXPECTED: SKILL.md Python examples use open() and os.makedirs() to write JSON output files and create directories. These are standard Python I/O operations for saving retrieved NCBI data. The examples use proper UTF-8 encoding and ensure_ascii=False for JSON output. No arbitrary file access patterns detected. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: File operations are constrained to output directories created by the script itself, with no user-controlled path injection.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm detection (false positive)
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at 7 locations. All are references to the NCBI 'Entrez' database system name in documentation and code examples. No cryptographic operations, hashing, or encryption exist in any file. The word 'Entrez' likely triggered a crypto-related heuristic. Confidence: 1.0. Confidence reasoning: Direct file inspection confirms zero cryptographic operations across all scanned files.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance detection (false positive)
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: 5 findings flagged 'system reconnaissance' for phrases about batch processing, CLI flags, and caching. These are documentation best practices, not reconnaissance commands. Confidence: 0.95. Confidence reasoning: The flagged text discusses technical development patterns (parameter stacking, batch size, local caching) which are standard documentation topics.

low_findings.title: Network URLs and HTTP library references
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE but EXPECTED: SKILL.md and references/databases.md contain URLs to the NCBI website and GitHub source repository, plus Python HTTP library imports (urllib.error.HTTPError). These are legitimate references for the skill's domain of NCBI database access. The skill's purpose is making network calls to NCBI E-utilities. Confidence: 0.95. Confidence reasoning: All URLs point to legitimate NCBI domains (ncbi.nlm.nih.gov) and the skill's GitHub source. No suspicious endpoints detected.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation heuristic - dangerous combination (false positive)
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static analyzer flagged 'DANGEROUS COMBINATION: Code execution + Network + Credential access' and 'SUSPICIOUS COMBINATION: Filesystem + Credentials + Network' as heuristic obfuscation patterns. In context, each individual pattern is a legitimate part of Bio.Entrez documentation: Python code examples (not execution), NCBI API URLs (not credential exfiltration), and file output. The combination is inherent to the skill's scientific research purpose. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: Every component pattern has a documented legitimate explanation in the skill's domain of bioinformatics API access.

content.user_title: Search and retrieve NCBI biological databases with Biopython
content.value_statement: Researchers need programmatic access to NCBI databases like PubMed and GenBank but managing API calls, rate limits, and data parsing is complex. This skill provides clear guidance on using Bio.Entrez to search, fetch, and parse records from NCBI E-utilities with best practices for rate limiting and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Search NCBI databases (PubMed, GenBank, Protein, Gene, Taxonomy) using keyword queries and retrieve matching record IDs
Fetch full records in multiple formats (GenBank, FASTA, MEDLINE, XML) and parse them with Biopython parsers
Retrieve lightweight document summaries to decide which full records to download
Discover cross-database relationships using ELinks (e.g., PubMed to PMC, Gene to Protein)
Handle large result sets with batch processing and server-side history via WebEnv and QueryKey
Configure NCBI API keys and respect rate limits for reliable automated access
limitations: Requires an active internet connection to access NCBI servers
Limited by NCBI rate limits and API usage policies which may throttle requests
Does not include data analysis, visualization, or statistical processing of retrieved records
Requires basic Python and Biopython knowledge to write and modify scripts

use_cases.title: Automated literature review for systematic reviews
use_cases.description: Search PubMed by keyword, author, journal, or date range, retrieve abstracts and metadata, and export results as structured JSON for further analysis.
use_cases.target_user: Medical researchers and systematic review teams

use_cases.title: Batch sequence data collection for genomic analysis
use_cases.description: Download GenBank records in batch using accession IDs or search queries, retrieve sequences in FASTA or GenBank format for phylogenetic or genomic research.

use_cases.title: Cross-database reference linking for pathway analysis
use_cases.description: Find related records across NCBI databases (e.g., link nucleotide sequences to protein records to publications) to build comprehensive research datasets.

prompt_templates.title: Basic PubMed literature search
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to search PubMed for recent research articles about CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing published in 2023. Use Bio.Entrez to search the pubmed database, retrieve the top 10 matching article IDs, and then fetch their summaries including title, authors, and publication date. Write the results to a JSON file.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user searching for research articles

prompt_templates.title: Batch GenBank sequence download
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a list of GenBank accession numbers for mitochondrial genome sequences. Use Bio.Entrez to fetch each sequence in GenBank format, parse them with SeqIO, and save each record to a separate .gb file. Include error handling for invalid accession numbers.
prompt_templates.scenario: User with existing accession list needs sequence data

prompt_templates.title: Cross-database link analysis from gene to publications
prompt_templates.prompt: Search NCBI for the human BRCA1 gene, then use ELink to find associated protein records and PubMed publications. Retrieve summaries for the linked publications and compile a report showing how the gene connects to proteins and research articles.

prompt_templates.title: Large-scale literature retrieval with server-side history
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a broad PubMed search for 'cancer genomics' with a result set exceeding 1000 articles. Use WebEnv and QueryKey to manage the search on NCBI servers. Fetch results in batches of 200 using the server-side history to avoid sending large ID lists. Add rate-limit-aware delays between requests.
prompt_templates.scenario: Power user handling large-scale data extraction

output_examples.input: Search PubMed for CRISPR Cas9 articles published in 2020 and retrieve titles and authors.
output_examples.output: The skill generates a JSON file containing matching article summaries. Each entry includes the PubMed ID (PMID), article title, list of authors with last name and initials, journal name, and publication date. The output is formatted as structured JSON with a query field, count field, and items array for easy programmatic access.

output_examples.input: Download GenBank sequences for three mitochondrial genome accession numbers.
output_examples.output: The skill produces individual GenBank format (.gb) files for each accession number. Each file contains the complete GenBank record including the sequence header, organism information, gene annotations, and the full nucleotide sequence. Records are parsed using Bio.SeqIO for proper GenBank format compliance.

output_examples.input: Find all PubMed articles linked to a specific nucleotide sequence in GenBank.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a cross-reference report showing the nucleotide accession ID, linked PubMed article IDs with titles and authors, and the relationship type (e.g., 'nucleotide_protein' or 'nucleotide_pubmed'). Results are organized by link name for clear traceability between databases.

best_practices: Always set Entrez.email to your email address as required by NCBI usage policies before making any API calls
Configure an NCBI API key to increase rate limits from 3 to 10 requests per second for faster data retrieval
Use batch processing with retstart and retmax parameters for large result sets to avoid overloading the server
anti_patterns: Sending too many rapid requests without delays, which can trigger rate limiting and temporary IP blocks from NCBI servers
Fetching full records without first reviewing summaries, leading to unnecessary bandwidth and processing time
Hardcoding API keys directly in scripts instead of using configuration files or environment variables

faq.question: Is an NCBI API key required to use Bio.Entrez?
faq.answer: No, an API key is not required. Without one your request rate is limited to 3 requests per second. With an API key the limit increases to 10 requests per second.

faq.question: What databases does Bio.Entrez support?
faq.answer: Bio.Entrez supports all NCBI Entrez databases including PubMed, GenBank, Nucleotide, Protein, Gene, Taxonomy, SNP, SRA, and many more.

faq.question: Can I use this skill without an internet connection?
faq.answer: No, Bio.Entrez requires an active internet connection to communicate with NCBI servers for all search and fetch operations.

faq.question: What Python and Biopython versions are needed?
faq.answer: This skill requires Python 3.7 or higher with Biopython version 1.80 or newer. Check your versions with python --version and python -c 'import Bio; print(Bio.__version__)'.

faq.question: How do I obtain an NCBI API key?
faq.answer: Create an NCBI account at the NCBI website, go to your account settings, and generate an API key. The key is a string you include in your Entrez.api_key configuration.

faq.question: What output formats are available for fetched records?
faq.answer: Formats depend on the database. Common options include FASTA and GenBank for sequences, MEDLINE and XML for publications, and XML for gene and taxonomy records.
